title: Why Rate Changes Take Time
objective: Explain what the base rate is and why a change in it acts on the economy only after long and variable lags.

sources.title: Bank of England — Monetary policy and how it works
sources.trust_rationale: Central bank — primary authority on its own policy mechanism.

beats.narration: Let's start with the base rate. It's the interest rate a central bank, like the Bank of England, pays to commercial banks. It sets the floor that everyone else's rates are built on.

visual.alt: Base rate sits beneath the rates banks charge households and businesses

beats.narration: When the central bank raises the base rate, borrowing becomes more expensive. So households and businesses borrow and spend a little less. That gentle cooling of demand is the main lever used to bring inflation down.

visual.alt: A higher rate pushing the demand curve downward

beats.narration: But the effect is not instant. Loans, wages and prices only adjust gradually, so a rate change today can take one to two years to fully bite. Economists call these long and variable lags.

visual.alt: A timeline showing the effect of a rate change building over about two years

beats.narration_intro: Quick check before we move on.
beats.prompt: Why don't rate changes move prices straight away?

options.text: Loans, wages and prices all adjust only gradually
Banks are legally required to wait a fixed period
Prices are reset by the government once each year

beats.correct_feedback: Right. Because contracts, loans, wages and prices adjust slowly, the full effect of a rate change arrives only after long and variable lags.
beats.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. The answer is that loans, wages and prices all adjust only gradually — a rate change works through the economy with a time lag, so its full effect lands only after long and variable delays.
